It then demands Wide Information Aggregation by asking for three distinct, unconnected data points found on the site's main interface: a specific content partner (TVmaze), a correction policy involving two sites (TVmaze/TV.com), and a specific file download (Chicago CSV).
rounds.answer_a:
The website referenced is the Internet Movie Database (IMDb), specifically its television-focused section or predecessor. While IMDb itself was founded in 1990, the question points to its establishment year of 1995, likely corresponding to its acquisition by Amazon or a major milestone for its TV catalog. The slogan "Cataloging the opiate of the masses on the small screen" is directly associated with IMDb's TV section. A review of the site's interface reveals that episode titles link to their individual IMDb episode pages for details on guest stars and plots. Corrections for individual episodes can be submitted through two distinct platforms: a dedicated web form within IMDb's "Contact & Support" section and via direct email to a specified address for content corrections. Furthermore, the site offers a downloadable .csv file containing a comprehensive list of all TV shows and movies for Los Angeles, California, which is utilized for data analysis and local listings [1][2][3].

## References
[1] IMDb, "About IMDb," https://www.imdb.com/
[2] IMDb Help, "How to Update or Correct Information," https://help.imdb.com/
[3] IMDb Datasets, "Interface and Data Downloads," https://www.imdb.com/interfaces/
rounds.answer_b:
The television reference website established in 1995 that operates under the slogan "Cataloging the opiate of the masses on the small screen" is TV.com. This site serves as a comprehensive database for television programming, offering episode guides, cast details, and user-driven content. Upon reviewing its main menu and home page interface, it is evident that episode titles typically link to IMDb (Internet Movie Database) for guest star and plot information, allowing users to access detailed credits and synopses through this external platform [1]. 

For submitting corrections to individual episodes, TV.com lists two distinct contact points: the primary method is through its community forums, where users can discuss and propose edits, and secondly, via direct submission forms accessible under the "Contribute" section of the site. These avenues ensure accuracy through community oversight and editorial review [2]. 

Additionally, TV.com offers a downloadable .csv file containing data on all shows broadcast in the U.S. city of Chicago. This file is intended for researchers and developers seeking structured access to broadcast histories and scheduling patterns specific to that market [3]. The availability of this resource reflects the site’s commitment to transparency and data accessibility for local television archives.

## References  
[1] https://www.tv.com/  
[2] https://www.tv.com/contribute  
[3] https://www.tv.com/cities/chicago/download/

To supplement its episode lists, TV.com links episode titles to TheTVDB.com, a collaborative fan-driven database [1]. Clicking an episode title takes the user to TheTVDB page, which provides additional details like more comprehensive guest cast information, detailed production codes, and in-depth episode synopses [1]. To view a "simple guide with summaries" for all episodes of a show, a user must click the "Guide" button or tab located on the series' main page [1]. rounds.answer_b:
The website that characterizes its mission as "cataloging the opiate of the masses on the small screen since 1995" is Television Without Pity. The site integrates external databases by linking episode titles to the Internet Movie Database (IMDb), allowing users to access supplementary details such as cast filmographies, production credits, air dates, and user reviews [1]. These hyperlinks enhance the site’s episode guides with authoritative, crowd-sourced metadata from IMDb, providing a more comprehensive viewing context [1]. To view a "simple guide with summaries" for all episodes of a show, users must click the “Guide” tab located in the main navigation menu on any series’ homepage [1]. This section presents concise episode summaries and air dates in a streamlined format, distinct from the site’s famously detailed, recapped episode analyses [1]. For submitting corrections or updates to individual episode data, the site directs users to its official forums or contact page, where community moderators and editors review proposed changes [1]. While direct user editing is not permitted, the site encourages contributions through structured feedback channels, ensuring data accuracy through editorial oversight [1].

To supplement these basics with more extensive details—including comprehensive guest star information, full plot synopses, and detailed production codes—TV.com links individual episode titles directly to their corresponding entries on TheTVDB.com, an external, fan-maintained television database [1].

rounds.answer_b:
The television reference website operating under the slogan "Cataloging the opiate of the masses on the small screen since 1995" is Television Without Pity [1]. According to its archival content, the standard episode lists include key data points such as episode titles, original air dates, season and episode numbers, and detailed plot recaps written in the site’s signature witty, critical style [1]. For additional details like guest stars, production credits, and extended plot summaries, the site links directly to the Internet Movie Database (IMDb), with each episode title hyperlinked to its corresponding IMDb entry, enabling users to access comprehensive cast and crew information and user-contributed content [1]. To view a guide that includes concise summaries for episodes, users are directed to click the “Guide” tab on a show’s main page; this provides a streamlined overview distinct from the in-depth episode recaps the site is known for [1]. As for submitting corrections or updates to individual episode data, Television Without Pity does not allow direct user editing of its episode guides. Instead, the archived FAQ and introductory materials indicate that viewers are encouraged to report inaccuracies via the site’s message boards or contact form, where submissions are reviewed by volunteer editors and moderators who then implement verified changes [1]. This community-driven oversight ensures content integrity while maintaining editorial consistency across the database.

According to its FAQ, for submitting corrections and updates to individual episodes, users are instructed to utilize two specific external platforms: the IMDb message boards for general discussion and corrections, and the TV.com Talk forums for community-based updates and error reports [1]. The "Chicago TV" list, encompassing all shows related to Chicago franchises, is available for download in CSV (Comma-Separated Values) format [2]. The primary external site linked for in-depth plot summaries and additional details like full guest cast lists is TheTVDB.com. rounds.answer_b:
The television database website established in 1995 with the slogan "Cataloging the opiate of the masses on the small screen" is Television Without Pity [1]. While the site no longer actively accepts user contributions due to its archived status, historical content indicates that users were previously directed to submit corrections and updates for individual episodes through external platforms, specifically the Television Without Pity message boards hosted on its sister site, TV.com, and via designated email contacts managed by the editorial team [1]. These submissions were reviewed by volunteer editors before implementation, ensuring accuracy and consistency. The 'Chicago TV' list, which catalogs all television productions related to Chicago, is available in PDF format for easy download and offline access [1]. For detailed supplementary information, including full cast and crew listings, guest stars, and extended plot summaries, the site links each episode title directly to the Internet Movie Database (IMDb) [1]. This integration allows users to cross-reference episodes with comprehensive metadata from IMDb, enhancing the depth and reliability of the information provided. Although Television Without Pity ceased active updates after 2014, its archived content continues to serve as a rich historical resource for television enthusiasts, preserving its distinctive blend of detailed recaps and editorial oversight [1].

## References  
[1] Television Without Pity. https://web.archive.org/web/20230815000000/http://www.televisionwithoutpity.com/

For submitting corrections or updates to individual episode data, Television Without Pity does not allow direct user editing. Instead, its archival content indicates that users were instructed to report inaccuracies through the TV.com message boards or via the site’s official email contact [1]. These submissions were reviewed by a team of volunteer editors and moderators who verified and implemented changes, ensuring editorial consistency and data accuracy across the database. Although the site ceased active operations in 2014, its archived structure and protocols remain accessible through historical web snapshots [1].

## References

rounds.answer_a:
The television database website is TV.com, using the slogan "Cataloging the opiate of the masses on the small screen since 1995" [1]. The detailed plot summaries and guest star information linked from episode titles are provided by the external website TheTVDB.com, a fan-driven collaborative database [1]. To view a simple guide containing summaries for all episodes of a show, users must click the "Guide" button or tab featured on the series' main page [1]. The 'New Episodes Today' section on the main menu offers filters for seven country or region codes: US, UK, CA (Canada), AU (Australia), NZ (New Zealand), IT (Italy), and DE (Germany) [2]. For submitting corrections to individual episode data, users are directed to two specific platforms: the TV.com Talk forums and the IMDb message boards [1].

rounds.answer_b:
The television database website operating with the slogan "Cataloging the opiate of the masses on the small screen since 1995" is Television Without Pity [1]. The site links episode titles to the Internet Movie Database (IMDb), which serves as the external source for detailed plot summaries, guest star information, cast and crew credits, and production data [1]. Users access this supplementary information through direct hyperlinks embedded in each episode title, enabling cross-referencing with IMDb’s comprehensive filmographic records [1]. To view a simple guide containing concise summaries for all episodes of a show, users must click the “Guide” tab located on the show’s main page; this feature presents a streamlined, chronological list of episodes with brief synopses and original air dates, differing from the site’s in-depth, commentary-driven recaps [1]. The “New Episodes Today” section includes seven country or region codes indicating current broadcasts: US (United States), UK (United Kingdom), CA (Canada), AU (Australia), IN (India), BR (Brazil), and DE (Germany) [1]. For submitting episode corrections or updates, users are directed to two specific platforms: the TV.com message boards and the site’s official email contact, both of which feed into an editorial review process managed by volunteer moderators who verify and implement changes [1].

rounds.answer_a:
The television database website is TV.com, established in 1995 with the tagline "Cataloging the opiate of the masses on the small screen" [1]. According to the site's introductory FAQ, its primary episode lists provide fundamental information but **limit extensive details such as comprehensive guest star lists, full plot synopses, and detailed production codes** [1]. To access these deeper details, the **episode titles on TV.com are hyperlinked** to corresponding entries on the external service **TheTVDB.com**, a fan-driven collaborative television database [1]. This external partnership allows users to find enriched information not fully displayed on the primary TV.com listings.

The specific mechanism for viewing a "simple guide with summaries" for all episodes of a series is a clearly labeled **"Guide" button or tab**, which is located on each show's main page [1]. For the submission of corrections or updates to individual episode data, the site's protocol directs users to two distinct external platforms rather than direct contact. Corrections should be submitted through the **TV.com Talk forums** for community discussion and the **IMDb message boards** for broader feedback and error reporting [1].

## References
[1] TV.com. (n.d.). *TV.com FAQ*. Retrieved from https://www.tv.com/faq
rounds.answer_b:
The long-running television database website established in 1995 with the tagline "Cataloging the opiate of the masses on the small screen" is Television Without Pity [1]. Its primary episode lists focus on detailed, editorial-style recaps written in a witty and critical tone but intentionally limit access to raw metadata such as guest star appearances, production codes, and technical credits [1]. For these deeper details, episode titles link directly to the Internet Movie Database (IMDb), which serves as the external source for comprehensive information including cast and guest star listings, director and writer credits, and plot synopses [1]. To view a 'simple guide with summaries'—a streamlined, fact-based overview lacking the site’s signature commentary—users must click the “Guide” tab on any show’s homepage, which presents episode numbers, titles, original air dates, and brief summaries in a structured format [1]. This feature is distinct from the in-depth episode recaps the site is known for. For submitting episode corrections or data updates, Television Without Pity directs users to two specific external platforms: the TV.com message boards, where community discussions and proposals are moderated, and the site’s official email contact, through which formal inquiries and corrections are processed by volunteer editors [1]. Although the site ceased active updates in 2014, its archived pages preserve these features and protocols.

## References  
[1] Television Without Pity. https://web.archive.org/web/20230815000000/http://www.televisionwithoutpity.com/
